title: Botulism - NHS
text:
maged cans, and avoid eating foul-smelling preserved foods, foods stored at the incorrect temperature and out-of-date foods.
Infant botulism In many cases of infant botulism, the specific cause is not identified so it may not always be possible to prevent it.
But you should avoid giving honey to babies under the age of 1 as it's been known to contain Clostridium botulinum spores.
Wound botulism People who inject heroin can get botulism. This is often through injecting heroin, contaminated with the bacteria, into the skin or muscles.
The best way to avoid getting botulism is to stop using heroin. Find out more about how to get help for heroin addiction. 
GOV.UK has more information about how to reduce your risk of getting botulism if you do inject heroin 
Iatrogenic botulism It's rare to get botulism from the toxin being used for medical or cosmetic procedures.
You should check with the person doing your procedure that the product they're using is licensed and has been prescribed by a doctor or qualified healthcare professional.
Making sure you see someone who's trained and qualified to do cosmetic procedures is also important.
Find out more about choosing who will do your cosmetic procedure
